decks.name: Hiawatha (Poem)
decks.description: Learn about the poem Hiawatha and the young Native American boy who loved nature

cards.back: Hiawatha is a young Native American boy who grew up in a forest. He loved nature and learned the language of birds and animals.
cards.hint: He is a boy from a forest

cards.back: Hiawatha was raised by his old grandmother, Nokomis, who taught him many things about the world around him.

cards.front: What did Hiawatha learn about birds?
cards.back: Hiawatha learned the names of all the birds. He learned their language, their secrets, where they built their nests, and how they hid in summer.
cards.hint: He knew their names and secrets

cards.front: What did Hiawatha learn about animals?
cards.back: Hiawatha learned the names of all the beasts (animals). He learned where they hid and where the beavers built their lodges.

cards.front: What is the main message of the poem 'Hiawatha'?
cards.back: The poem teaches us to love and respect nature. Hiawatha lived in harmony with birds and animals, understanding their ways and learning from them.
cards.hint: It is about living with nature

decks.name: The Scholar's Mother Tongue (Story)
decks.description: Learn about Birbal's cleverness and the importance of one's mother tongue

cards.back: Birbal was one of the nine gems (navratnas) in Emperor Akbar's court. He was known for his great wisdom and cleverness.

cards.front: What challenge did Emperor Akbar give to his courtiers?
cards.back: Emperor Akbar asked his courtiers to find out the mother tongue of a learned scholar (pandit) who could speak many languages fluently.
cards.hint: It was about a scholar who knew many languages

cards.front: Why was it difficult to find the scholar's mother tongue?
cards.back: The scholar spoke every language so fluently that no one could tell which was his mother tongue. He spoke each language as if it were his own.

cards.front: How did Birbal discover the scholar's mother tongue?
cards.back: Birbal went to the scholar's room at night when he was asleep and gently tickled his ear. When startled from sleep, the scholar cried out in his mother tongue!

cards.front: What lesson does the story teach us?
cards.back: The story teaches us that no matter how many languages a person learns, when taken by surprise, they always react in their mother tongue. It shows the deep connection we have with our first language.
cards.hint: About the bond with our first language

decks.name: Grammar: Pronouns
decks.description: Learn about pronouns and how they replace nouns in sentences

cards.back: A pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun. Examples: he, she, it, they, we, I, you. Pronouns help us avoid repeating the same noun again and again.

cards.front: Replace the noun with a pronoun: 'Birbal was very clever. Birbal solved the puzzle.'
cards.back: 'Birbal was very clever. He solved the puzzle.' Here, the pronoun 'He' replaces the noun 'Birbal'.
cards.hint: Use a pronoun for a boy or man

cards.front: Which pronoun would you use for Hiawatha's grandmother Nokomis?
cards.back: We would use 'she' or 'her' for Nokomis because she is a woman. Example: 'Nokomis told Hiawatha many stories. She loved him dearly.'

cards.front: What is the difference between 'he' and 'him'?
cards.back: 'He' is used when the person is doing the action (subject). 'Him' is used when the action is done to the person (object). Example: 'He solved the puzzle.' vs. 'Akbar praised him.'
cards.hint: One does the action, one receives it

cards.front: Choose the correct pronoun: 'The birds sang sweetly. ___ built nests in the trees.'
cards.back: 'They' is the correct pronoun. 'The birds sang sweetly. They built nests in the trees.' We use 'they' for more than one person, animal, or thing.
